h1Title: Psoriatic Arthritis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory arthritis that develops in roughly 20-30% of people with psoriasis, producing painful, swollen, and progressively damaged joints alongside skin and nail disease. It affects approximately 0.05-0.25% of the general population and 0.5-1% of those of European descent, with peak onset between ages 30 and 50 and equal sex distribution. Most patients have established psoriasis for 7-10 years before joint disease begins, but in 15-20% the arthritis precedes the rash. Characteristic features include asymmetric oligoarthritis, dactylitis (sausage digits), enthesitis (inflammation at tendon-bone insertions), axial involvement (spondylitis and sacroiliitis), and distinctive nail changes (pitting, onycholysis). Diagnosed using the CASPAR criteria, psoriatic arthritis responds to a hierarchy of treatments — NSAIDs, conventional DMARDs (methotrexate, leflunomide), targeted biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17, IL-23, IL-12/23), and oral small molecules (apremilast, JAK inhibitors) — that achieve substantial response in 50-70% of patients and slow joint destruction.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 0.05-0.25% of the general population; 20-30% of patients with psoriasis develop PsA (Alinaghi et al. 2019 meta-analysis)
keyStats.demographics: Roughly equal sex distribution; peak age 30-50; higher prevalence in white European populations
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at onset 36-50 years; symptoms typically begin 7-10 years after psoriasis onset, but PsA precedes skin disease in 15-20%
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 14-25 million people globally with psoriatic arthritis (extrapolation from prevalence data)

definition: Psoriatic arthritis (ICD-10: L40.50-L40.59) is a heterogeneous immune-mediated inflammatory arthritis classified within the seronegative spondyloarthritis family and clinically associated with psoriasis of the skin or nails. Pathogenesis involves dysregulated innate and adaptive immunity in genetically susceptible individuals: HLA-Cw0602 and HLA-B27 confer significant risk, and downstream pathways involving IL-23 / Th17, IL-17A, and TNF-alpha drive synovitis, enthesitis, and bone remodeling. The Moll and Wright classification originally described five clinical patterns: distal interphalangeal predominant, asymmetric oligoarticular, symmetric polyarticular (rheumatoid-like), arthritis mutilans, and predominant spondylitis. Most patients display overlapping features rather than pure patterns. CASPAR (Classification Criteria for Psoriatic Arthritis, 2006) operationalizes diagnosis: patients require established inflammatory articular disease (joint, spine, or entheseal) plus at least 3 points from psoriasis (current 2, history 1, family history 1), nail changes, dactylitis (current 1, history 1), seronegativity (rheumatoid factor negative 1), and juxta-articular new bone formation on radiographs (1). The disease causes structural joint damage in 40-60% within 2-3 years if untreated, irreversible deformity in advanced cases, and substantial functional impairment. Beyond joint and skin manifestations, patients face elevated risks of cardiovascular disease, uveitis, inflammatory bowel disease, metabolic syndrome, depression, and fatty liver. Care is led by rheumatology in close collaboration with dermatology, ophthalmology, gastroenterology, and primary care.

typesClassification.description: Inflammation concentrated in distal finger or toe joints, frequently with adjacent nail changes (pitting, onycholysis). About 5% of cases historically; highly characteristic of PsA and rare in rheumatoid arthritis.

Fewer than 5 joints involved, asymmetric pattern. Affects 30-50% of PsA patients. Often associated with dactylitis and enthesitis. Tends to involve large lower-limb joints (knees, ankles) and distal upper limbs.

Affects 30-50% of patients. Symmetric small-joint disease resembling rheumatoid arthritis. Distinguished from RA by seronegativity, accompanying psoriasis, DIP involvement, dactylitis, and radiographic features of both erosion and new bone formation.
Severe destructive arthritis with osteolysis producing 'telescoping' digits and pencil-in-cup deformities. Affects under 5% but causes the most disability. Aggressive immunomodulation required.
Axial PsA with inflammatory back pain, sacroiliac joint inflammation, and progression to syndesmophyte formation. Occurs in 25-50% of PsA at some point. Often coexists with peripheral disease.

primarySymptoms: Joint pain, stiffness, swelling, and tenderness most marked in the morning and after rest, lasting more than 30 minutes (inflammatory pattern).
Asymmetric joint involvement, often beginning in a single knee, ankle, finger, or toe and spreading over weeks to months.
Distal interphalangeal joint swelling adjacent to a nail with characteristic pitting or onycholysis — highly specific for PsA.
Dactylitis (sausage digit): diffuse uniform swelling of an entire finger or toe with pain, warmth, and reduced movement, present at some point in 30-50% of patients.
Enthesitis: pain and tenderness at tendon-bone insertions, classically the Achilles insertion, plantar fascia, lateral epicondyle, or iliac crest.
Inflammatory back pain — pain and stiffness in the low back and buttocks that improves with movement and worsens with rest — in 25-50% with axial involvement.
Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis (lifting of nail from nail bed), oil drop sign, subungual hyperkeratosis, splinter hemorrhages — present in 80-90% of PsA patients.
Active or past psoriasis of the skin or scalp (plaque, guttate, pustular, or inverse pattern), often pre-dating arthritis by 7-10 years.
Fatigue, sleep disturbance, and reduced exercise tolerance, partly from inflammation and partly from chronic pain.
Reduced grip strength, hand or foot dexterity, and impaired performance of activities of daily living.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent morning stiffness in the fingers or toes lasting more than 30 minutes in a person with known psoriasis
A single swollen finger or toe (dactylitis) — sausage digit appearance
Heel pain at the back or under the foot in someone with psoriasis or scalp psoriasis
New nail pitting, lifting, or yellow-brown spots on nails
Inflammatory low-back pain in a young adult with psoriasis or strong family history
emergencySigns: Acute red eye with pain, photophobia, and reduced vision — exclude acute anterior uveitis (urgent ophthalmology assessment within 24 hours)
Fever, severely swollen single joint, and inability to bear weight — exclude septic arthritis with urgent joint aspiration and culture
Sudden new severe back pain with neurological signs (limb weakness, bladder dysfunction) — exclude cauda equina or fracture
Skin pustules covering large body areas with fever — generalized pustular psoriasis is a medical emergency
Severe abdominal pain with blood in stool — exclude superimposed inflammatory bowel disease flare

causes.description: PsA has strong genetic basis with sibling recurrence risk roughly 30 times that of the general population. HLA-Cw0602 carries the strongest skin-disease risk; HLA-B27 carries the strongest axial-disease risk. Other associated alleles include HLA-B38, HLA-B39, and HLA-B*0801. Non-HLA loci (IL23R, TRAF3IP2, TNIP1, NOS2) contribute to immune pathway dysregulation.

causes.cause: Immune dysregulation through IL-23/Th17 and TNF pathways
causes.description: IL-23 from antigen-presenting cells activates Th17 cells producing IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-22, and TNF-alpha. These cytokines drive synovitis, enthesitis, bone resorption (osteoclast activation), and pathological bone formation. Innate lymphoid cells and gamma-delta T cells at entheses are also implicated. This pathway directly informs modern targeted biologic therapy.

causes.cause: Mechanical stress and microtrauma at entheses
causes.description: The 'deep Koebner' phenomenon describes how mechanical stress at tendon-bone insertions can trigger local enthesitis in genetically predisposed individuals. This explains the predilection for Achilles, plantar fascia, and weight-bearing joints, and why occupational repetitive trauma can flare disease.

Altered skin and gut microbiomes in PsA produce immune-stimulating metabolites and antigens. Streptococcal infection triggers guttate psoriasis and sometimes new PsA. Gut dysbiosis and increased intestinal permeability are documented in 10-15% of patients, contributing to systemic inflammation.

Physical trauma to a joint (Koebner phenomenon), recent infection (streptococcal, viral), psychological stress, smoking, and certain medications (beta-blockers, lithium, antimalarials) can precipitate disease onset or flare in genetically susceptible individuals.

riskFactors.description: 20-30% of patients with psoriasis develop PsA. Risk is highest in those with severe skin disease, nail involvement, and scalp psoriasis. Nail psoriasis confers 3-fold risk of PsA.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of psoriasis or psoriatic arthritis
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of PsA patients have 30-fold increased risk versus the general population. Twin concordance rates support strong genetic contribution.
HLA-Cw0602 is the strongest skin-disease risk allele; HLA-B27 is the strongest axial-disease risk allele. Testing is not routine for diagnosis but informs prognosis in specialist settings.

riskFactors.factor: Severe psoriasis with extensive body-surface involvement
riskFactors.description: Patients with PASI > 10 have 2-3 times the PsA risk of those with mild psoriasis. Aggressive skin treatment may reduce subsequent PsA development.
Obesity raises PsA risk in psoriasis patients by approximately 50-100% and reduces response to all therapies. Each unit of BMI above 25 raises risk by 5-7%.
Active and prior smoking increase PsA risk by 30-50% in some cohorts and reduce response to TNF inhibitors. Smoking cessation improves disease outcomes.
Physical injury to a joint can trigger local PsA onset in genetically predisposed individuals. Recreational athletes, manual workers, and recent surgical patients with psoriasis are watch groups.
Streptococcal pharyngitis can trigger guttate psoriasis and new PsA in susceptible individuals. Prompt treatment of streptococcal infection in psoriasis patients is recommended.

affectedDemographics: Patients with established psoriasis: 20-30% develop PsA over lifetime.
White European descent: prevalence 0.2-0.3%; lower in East Asian (0.05-0.1%) and African populations.
Equal sex distribution overall; axial disease slightly male-predominant, polyarticular disease slightly female-predominant.
Peak onset age 30-50, with smaller pediatric peak (juvenile PsA).
Patients with severe psoriasis, scalp or inverse psoriasis, nail involvement, and family history are over-represented.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical pattern recognition with the CASPAR classification criteria. A patient with inflammatory joint, spine, or entheseal disease receives 1 CASPAR point each for current psoriasis (2 points), history of psoriasis, family history of psoriasis, current dactylitis (1 point), history of dactylitis, typical nail changes, juxta-articular new bone formation on radiographs, and negative rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP. Three or more points confirm PsA classification with 99% specificity. Clinical assessment captures the 68 tender and 66 swollen joint counts, dactylitis count, Leeds Enthesitis Index or MASES enthesitis score, BASDAI for axial symptoms, PsA-specific composite scores (DAPSA, MDA, PASDAS), psoriasis severity (PASI, BSA), and patient-reported outcomes (HAQ, fatigue, sleep). Laboratory work-up includes complete blood count, CRP, ESR (elevated in 40-50%, normal in mild disease), rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP (negative in 90-95%), HLA-B27 (positive in 25-40%, especially axial PsA), uric acid, kidney and liver function, vitamin D, and screening for hepatitis B and C, tuberculosis (QuantiFERON or PPD), and HIV before biologic therapy. Imaging includes hand and foot radiographs (juxta-articular new bone, fluffy periosteal reaction, erosions, pencil-in-cup deformities, ankylosis), sacroiliac and lumbar spine X-ray (sacroiliitis, syndesmophytes), and increasingly ultrasound and MRI for early disease — ultrasound detects subclinical synovitis, enthesitis, and tenosynovitis; MRI is the most sensitive modality for axial inflammation and early peripheral enthesitis. Cardiovascular risk assessment (lipid panel, blood pressure, fasting glucose), depression screening, and ophthalmology baseline are recommended at diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical assessment with CASPAR criteria and joint/enthesitis counts
diagnosticTests.purpose: Operationalizes diagnosis (CASPAR 99% specific); tracks baseline severity (DAPSA, MDA, PASDAS)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute rheumatology consultation with full joint count, dactylitis assessment, enthesitis scoring (Leeds or MASES), psoriasis assessment (PASI or BSA), and patient-reported outcome measures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in 40-50% of PsA, particularly polyarticular and axial disease; tracks treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw with results within 24 hours. Normal markers do not exclude PsA.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes rheumatoid arthritis; negative in 90-95% of PsA (CASPAR criterion)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw. Results within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports axial PsA diagnosis; positive in 25-40% of PsA patients, particularly with sacroiliitis and ankylosing spondylitis-like pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw, results in 5-10 days. Not required for all patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects juxta-articular new bone formation, erosions, pencil-in-cup deformities, and joint space narrowing characteristic of PsA
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute outpatient X-ray of both hands and feet. Results within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Sacroiliac joint and lumbar spine radiographs or MRI
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies sacroiliitis and axial PsA; MRI detects active inflammation before structural change
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: X-ray takes 10-15 minutes; MRI 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner. Painless. Gadolinium contrast occasionally used.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects subclinical synovitis, enthesitis, tenosynovitis, and erosions; useful in early or borderline cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minute outpatient scan. Real-time imaging with the radiologist or rheumatologist; results immediate.

diagnosticTests.test: Pre-biologic screening: hepatitis B, hepatitis C, tuberculosis, HIV
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies infections that must be treated before initiating immunomodulatory therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood tests and tuberculosis screen (QuantiFERON-Gold or PPD). Results within 1-2 weeks. Required by guidelines before TNF inhibitor or IL-17/23 biologic initiation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows treat-to-target principles per GRAPPA 2021 and EULAR 2023 recommendations: aim for minimal disease activity or remission, with regular reassessment every 3-6 months and stepwise escalation. Step 1: NSAIDs and local intra-articular corticosteroid for mild peripheral disease. Step 2: conventional synthetic DMARDs (methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly first-line; leflunomide 20 mg daily or sulfasalazine 2-3 g daily as alternatives) for peripheral disease with elevated activity, prognostic factors, or NSAID failure. Conventional DMARDs do not work well for axial disease, enthesitis, or dactylitis. Step 3: targeted therapy when conventional DMARDs fail or are contraindicated, or up-front in severe disease, axial involvement, enthesitis, or dactylitis. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab) are first-line targeted therapy with 50-70% ACR20 response and proven inhibition of radiographic progression. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab) and IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab) are first-line or alternative options with excellent skin and joint responses and acceptable safety; IL-23 inhibitors particularly effective for skin. IL-12/23 inhibitor ustekinumab is effective but with somewhat lower joint response than TNF and IL-17 inhibitors. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib 5 mg twice daily, upadacitinib 15 mg daily) are oral options with broad efficacy; concerns about venous thromboembolism, MACE, and malignancy in older patients with cardiovascular risk factors limit first-line use per EMA and FDA 2022 guidance. Apremilast (phosphodiesterase-4 inhibitor) is a moderately effective oral option used in mild-to-moderate disease, attractive for its safety profile and lack of pre-screening requirements. Treatment for axial disease prefers TNF inhibitors and IL-17 inhibitors (conventional DMARDs ineffective). Skin disease is co-managed with dermatology; topical therapy, phototherapy, and biologics are coordinated. Treat-to-target with regular DAPSA or MDA assessment and dose adjustment improves outcomes. Comorbidity management (cardiovascular risk, depression, weight, smoking, vaccination, screening) is integral to long-term care.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (15-25 mg oral or subcutaneous, weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line conventional DMARD for peripheral PsA. Folic acid 1-5 mg weekly minimizes side-effects. Liver function and CBC monitored every 1-3 months. Effective for peripheral arthritis and dactylitis but less effective for enthesitis and axial disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response approximately 30-45%; meaningful response for skin in 20-40%. Less effective than biologics overall but cost-effective first-line for milder disease

medicalTreatments.name: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks; etanercept 50 mg SC weekly; infliximab 5 mg/kg IV every 8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line targeted biologic for moderate-to-severe PsA. Inhibits radiographic progression. Excellent efficacy for joint and skin disease. Pre-screening for tuberculosis, hepatitis B/C, and HIV required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response 50-70%, ACR50 30-50%, PASI75 50-70%; significant inhibition of radiographic progression (ADEPT, IMPACT, GO-REVEAL trials)

medicalTreatments.name: IL-17A inhibitors (secukinumab 150-300 mg SC; ixekizumab 80-160 mg SC; bimekizumab 160 mg SC every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Selective IL-17A or IL-17A/F inhibition. Excellent for skin and joint disease. Used first-line or after TNF inhibitor inadequate response. Caution in inflammatory bowel disease (relative contraindication).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response 55-70%, ACR50 30-45%, PASI90 65-80% (FUTURE-1/2, SPIRIT-P1/P2, BE OPTIMAL trials)

medicalTreatments.name: IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab 100 mg SC; risankizumab 150 mg SC at weeks 0, 4, then every 12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Selective IL-23 inhibition with excellent skin response and durable joint response. Particularly favorable safety profile and convenient dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response 55-65%, ACR50 30-40%, PASI90 75-85% (DISCOVER-1/2, KEEPsAKE-1/2 trials); response sustained at 2+ years

medicalTreatments.name: JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib 5 mg BID; upadacitinib 15 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral targeted small molecules. Broad efficacy for peripheral and axial disease, dactylitis, enthesitis, and skin. Pre-screening for tuberculosis, hepatitis, and lipids. Limit in patients over 65, smokers, or cardiovascular high-risk per FDA boxed warning (MACE, VTE, malignancy).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response 50-65%, ACR50 30-45%, PASI75 50-65% (OPAL-Beyond, SELECT-PsA trials)

medicalTreatments.name: Apremilast (30 mg BID oral)
medicalTreatments.description: Phosphodiesterase-4 inhibitor. Moderate efficacy with strong safety profile, no pre-screening or monitoring requirements. Used for mild-to-moderate disease and patients preferring oral therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACR20 response 30-40%, PASI75 30-40% (PALACE trials); milder GI side-effects and weight loss common

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular and entheseal corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Direct injection of methylprednisolone or triamcinolone for individual swollen joints or persistent enthesitis. Provides rapid local control. Avoid injection through psoriatic plaques.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% symptomatic improvement lasting 6-12 weeks; not a long-term strategy

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg BID, ibuprofen 400-800 mg TID, etoricoxib 60-90 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for mild peripheral or axial symptoms while initiating disease-modifying therapy. GI and cardiovascular risk evaluated before chronic use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides symptomatic relief; does not modify disease progression

surgicalOptions.name: Total hip or knee arthroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for severe destructive disease with refractory pain and functional impairment. Modern biologic therapy has reduced arthroplasty rates substantially.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 90-95% successful pain relief and functional improvement at 10 years; infection rates slightly higher than osteoarthritis controls due to immunosuppression

surgicalOptions.name: Tendon repair or reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Achilles or finger tendon ruptures associated with severe enthesitis may require surgical repair. Timing coordinated with biologic therapy schedule.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional recovery in 70-85%; risk of re-rupture and infection persists

surgicalOptions.name: Synovectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely performed in modern era; reserved for persistent isolated joint synovitis refractory to medication and intra-articular injection. Arthroscopic preferred when feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable; limited evidence in PsA-specific cohorts

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean diet and weight loss
alternativeTreatments.description: 10-15% body weight reduction in obese patients improves disease activity and treatment response. Mediterranean dietary pattern reduces inflammation and cardiovascular risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation
alternativeTreatments.description: Stopping smoking reduces disease activity and improves response to TNF inhibitors. Smoking is a modifiable factor for both onset and treatment response.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aerobic and resistance exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Regular exercise reduces pain, improves function, and addresses cardiovascular comorbidity. Supervised programs particularly effective in early disease.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a rheumatologist as soon as inflammatory joint, spine, or enthesis symptoms develop in a patient with psoriasis or family history. Early specialist referral and treat-to-target therapy reduce structural damage and disability. Concurrent dermatology, ophthalmology, gastroenterology, and cardiology input is needed for comorbidities.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist with experience in PsA, ideally in a multidisciplinary clinic with dermatology. Ask about use of treat-to-target (DAPSA, MDA scores), familiarity with all biologic and oral targeted therapies, and pathway for fast-tracking patients with new dactylitis or severe disease. Continuity of care over years matters because disease evolves and therapy needs adjustment.
hospitalCriteria: Rheumatology service with PsA expertise and ultrasound capability
Infusion suite for IV biologics (infliximab, IV golimumab)
Imaging (MRI, ultrasound, X-ray) and pre-biologic screening pathway
keyFacilities: Specialist PsA clinic with multidisciplinary input
Tertiary referral for refractory or complex PsA
preventionStrategies: If you have psoriasis, see a rheumatologist promptly for any joint pain, swelling, dactylitis, or persistent heel pain.
Maintain healthy weight (BMI under 25); each unit reduction improves treatment response and reduces disease activity.
Stop smoking to reduce disease activity and improve response to TNF inhibitors.
Control cardiovascular risk factors aggressively: blood pressure, lipids, blood glucose. PsA confers a 1.5-fold increase in cardiovascular events.
Get annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination; consider shingles vaccination (Shingrix) per guidelines.
Address skin disease promptly with appropriate topical, phototherapy, or systemic treatment in coordination with dermatology.
Treat streptococcal infections promptly in patients with psoriasis to limit triggering events.
lifestyleModifications: Take prescribed DMARDs and biologics exactly on schedule; missed doses or premature withdrawal cause flares and may produce drug-induced antibodies.
Attend laboratory monitoring (CBC, liver function, kidney function, lipids) every 3 months on methotrexate or JAK inhibitor.
Maintain regular low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling) and resistance training as tolerated.
Follow a Mediterranean-style anti-inflammatory diet rich in fish, vegetables, olive oil, and whole grains.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol intake (especially with methotrexate or JAK inhibitor use).
Practice good skin care with regular emollients; avoid harsh soaps and excessive UV.
Use joint protection techniques (ergonomic tools, splints, pacing) and consult occupational therapy for hand involvement.

recommended: Mediterranean diet rich in fish, vegetables, olive oil, nuts, whole grains, and legumes
Omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) or supplements (2-3 g/day EPA + DHA)
Foods rich in vitamin D (fatty fish, fortified dairy) and supplementation to maintain serum 25-OH-D above 30 ng/mL
Fiber-rich foods supporting gut microbiome health
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) supporting muscle mass during inflammation
avoid: Excessive alcohol — increases methotrexate hepatotoxicity and worsens metabolic risk
Refined sugars and ultra-processed foods that worsen inflammation and metabolic comorbidity
High-purine intake in patients with concurrent hyperuricemia
Smoking — accelerates disease and reduces biologic response

exerciseGuidelines: Combine moderate-intensity aerobic activity (30 minutes most days; cycling, swimming, walking) with resistance training (2-3 times weekly) and flexibility work. Patients with axial disease benefit from daily spinal mobility exercises and supervised hydrotherapy. Avoid high-impact activities during acute flares but resume promptly once active inflammation settles. Patients with hand involvement benefit from occupational therapy and joint-protection techniques. Exercise reduces pain, improves function, and addresses cardiovascular comorbidity.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed medications on schedule; biologic injections at home should be at the same time of week/day.
Apply prescribed topical therapy to psoriasis lesions; treat nail psoriasis with appropriate care.
Document new joint pain, swelling, or dactylitis in a symptom diary to discuss at appointments.
Perform daily stretching and flexibility work for affected joints; use heat for stiffness and ice for acute pain.
Manage fatigue with regular sleep, pacing of activities, and stress-management techniques.
Attend rheumatology visits every 3-6 months; emergency review if new uveitis, severe flare, or infection while on biologic.
prognosis: Outlook has transformed since the introduction of biologic therapy. With timely treat-to-target therapy, 50-70% of patients achieve ACR20 response, 30-50% achieve ACR50, and 20-30% reach minimal disease activity or remission. Radiographic progression is substantially slowed with TNF inhibitors and other biologics. Without treatment, 40-60% develop structural joint damage within 2-3 years, and 20-40% of untreated patients develop irreversible deformity over 10 years. Predictors of more severe disease include polyarticular onset, dactylitis, elevated CRP, structural damage at presentation, and longer symptom duration before diagnosis. Comorbidities — cardiovascular disease (1.5-fold increase), metabolic syndrome (40-50% prevalence), depression (15-25%), uveitis (5-10%), inflammatory bowel disease (5-7%), and fatty liver — substantially affect overall survival and quality of life. Modern care reduces excess cardiovascular mortality, but adherence to lifestyle, vaccination, and comorbidity screening is essential. Life expectancy in well-managed PsA is approaching that of the general population.
recoveryTimeline: NSAIDs and intra-articular steroid produce symptom relief in 24-72 hours. Conventional DMARDs (methotrexate) require 8-12 weeks for full effect. Biologics show meaningful improvement at 4-6 weeks with peak benefit by 12-24 weeks. Treat-to-target reassessment every 3-6 months guides escalation. Skin response often precedes joint response by 4-12 weeks with most biologics. Radiographic progression measurable on imaging at 6-12 months with appropriate therapy.
complications: Permanent joint deformity, ankylosis, and arthritis mutilans (under 5%) with irreversible disability.
Cardiovascular disease — PsA confers a 1.5-fold increased risk of myocardial infarction and stroke; managed with aggressive risk-factor control.
Uveitis (anterior or posterior) affecting 5-10% of PsA patients; can cause permanent vision loss without prompt ophthalmology care.
Inflammatory bowel disease in 5-7% of patients; coordinate with gastroenterology and avoid IL-17 inhibitors in active IBD.
Metabolic syndrome, fatty liver disease, depression, and reduced quality of life are highly prevalent comorbidities.
Infections related to biologic therapy: tuberculosis reactivation, hepatitis B reactivation, opportunistic infections; prevented by pre-screening and ongoing vigilance.

supportResources.name: National Psoriasis Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy with PsA-specific resources, support groups, and clinical-trial information.

supportResources.name: Psoriasis and Psoriatic Arthritis Alliance (PAPAA)
supportResources.description: UK patient charity with information, helpline, and clinician finder.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation — Psoriatic Arthritis
supportResources.description: US patient education, exercise guidance, and advocacy from the leading arthritis charity.

supportResources.name: GRAPPA — Group for Research and Assessment of Psoriasis and Psoriatic Arthritis
supportResources.description: Specialist clinician network with treatment guidelines and educational resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis typically produces symmetric small-joint polyarthritis with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies (60-80%) and no skin psoriasis or DIP joint involvement. Psoriatic arthritis is seronegative (95%), often asymmetric, includes DIP joints, dactylitis, enthesitis, nail changes, and accompanying psoriasis. Imaging shows new bone formation in PsA versus pure erosion in RA.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout produces acute monoarticular attacks (most often first metatarsophalangeal joint) with severe pain, redness, and warmth, hyperuricemia, and monosodium urate crystals on joint aspiration. PsA is more chronic, asymmetric, with multiple joints, dactylitis, enthesitis, and psoriasis. Both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis (with DIP involvement)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteoarthritis of the hands also affects DIP joints (Heberden's nodes) and PIP joints (Bouchard's nodes) but produces non-inflammatory bony enlargement, brief morning stiffness (under 30 minutes), and no nail psoriasis, dactylitis, or enthesitis. Inflammatory markers are normal. Radiographs show osteophytes without erosion or new bone formation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Reactive arthritis develops within 1-4 weeks of a gastrointestinal (Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia, Campylobacter) or genitourinary (Chlamydia) infection, with asymmetric oligoarthritis, enthesitis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis. Lacks psoriasis, although HLA-B27 positive in 70-90% and can mimic PsA. History of antecedent infection clarifies diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Skin disease that precedes PsA in 80% of cases and shares pathway-level pathophysiology. Co-managed with dermatology.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Main differential diagnosis; treatment strategies overlap but distinct features and trajectories.

relatedConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Other seronegative spondyloarthritis sharing HLA-B27 association and enthesitis features.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Can coexist or mimic PsA flares; managed with urate-lowering therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related axial spondyloarthritis with overlapping treatment principles.

faqs.question: What is psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis that develops in 20-30% of people with psoriasis, producing painful swollen joints, dactylitis (sausage digits), enthesitis (tendon-bone inflammation), nail changes, and sometimes spine involvement. Untreated, it causes progressive joint damage.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Early signs include morning joint stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, a single swollen finger or toe (dactylitis), heel pain, nail pitting or lifting, and new low-back pain in a person with psoriasis. Any of these in someone with psoriasis warrants rheumatology assessment.

faqs.question: How is psoriatic arthritis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses the CASPAR criteria: established inflammatory joint, spine, or enthesis disease plus at least 3 points from current psoriasis (2), history of psoriasis, family history, current dactylitis, history of dactylitis, nail changes, negative rheumatoid factor, and juxta-articular new bone formation on X-ray. Blood tests, X-rays, and sometimes MRI or ultrasound support diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on severity. Mild disease uses NSAIDs and intra-articular steroid. Moderate disease starts methotrexate. Moderate-to-severe disease or those with axial involvement, dactylitis, or enthesitis receive a biologic (TNF inhibitor, IL-17 inhibitor, or IL-23 inhibitor) or a JAK inhibitor. Treat-to-target therapy aims for minimal disease activity.

faqs.question: Can psoriatic arthritis be cured?
faqs.answer: Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic condition without a cure, but modern treatment achieves substantial remission or minimal disease activity in 50-70% of patients. Treat-to-target therapy with biologics or JAK inhibitors substantially slows joint damage and improves quality of life.

faqs.question: What is dactylitis?
faqs.answer: Dactylitis is uniform swelling of an entire finger or toe — a 'sausage digit' — caused by combined joint, tendon sheath, and entheseal inflammation. It is highly characteristic of psoriatic arthritis and other spondyloarthropathies, occurring in 30-50% of PsA patients. Sustained inflammation can cause permanent damage.

faqs.question: Does psoriatic arthritis only affect people with psoriasis?
faqs.answer: Most patients have established psoriasis before joint disease, but 15-20% develop arthritis before skin disease. A strong family history of psoriasis or psoriatic arthritis can support diagnosis even without overt skin disease. Nail changes alone can also be the only psoriasis-related feature.

faqs.question: Is psoriatic arthritis hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a strong genetic component. First-degree relatives of psoriatic arthritis patients have approximately 30-fold increased risk versus the general population. HLA-Cw0602 and HLA-B27 are the strongest known genetic risk factors, and several non-HLA genes contribute.

faqs.question: What is the difference between psoriatic arthritis and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Rheumatoid arthritis usually produces symmetric small-joint polyarthritis with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies and no skin psoriasis. Psoriatic arthritis is typically seronegative, includes DIP joints, dactylitis, enthesitis, nail changes, and accompanying psoriasis. Imaging shows new bone formation in PsA versus pure erosion in RA.

faqs.question: What are biologic treatments for psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Biologics target specific immune molecules: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab), IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab), IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab), and IL-12/23 inhibitor ustekinumab. They produce 50-70% ACR20 response and slow joint damage.

faqs.question: Are JAK inhibitors safe for psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) are effective oral options for PsA. However, FDA and EMA 2022 boxed warnings note increased cardiovascular events, venous thromboembolism, and malignancy in patients over 65, smokers, or with cardiovascular risk factors. Use is typically restricted to younger patients or after TNF inhibitor failure.

faqs.question: Does psoriatic arthritis cause disability?
faqs.answer: Untreated psoriatic arthritis causes structural joint damage in 40-60% within 2-3 years and significant disability in 20-40% over 10 years. Modern treat-to-target therapy substantially reduces this. Comorbid cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and depression contribute to reduced quality of life and require active management.

faqs.question: What is enthesitis in psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Enthesitis is inflammation at sites where tendons or ligaments attach to bone. Common sites include Achilles tendon insertion, plantar fascia, lateral epicondyle, and iliac crest. Enthesitis is a hallmark of PsA and other spondyloarthropathies. Biologics (TNF and IL-17 inhibitors) are particularly effective.

faqs.question: Can diet affect psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fish, vegetables, olive oil, and whole grains is associated with reduced disease activity. Obesity reduces treatment response — 10-15% weight loss improves outcomes substantially. Limit alcohol if taking methotrexate or JAK inhibitor. Specific supplements (vitamin D, omega-3) may help.

faqs.question: How does psoriatic arthritis affect the spine?
faqs.answer: Axial psoriatic arthritis affects 25-50% of patients, producing inflammatory back pain, sacroiliitis, and sometimes ankylosing-spondylitis-like fusion. Treatment uses TNF or IL-17 inhibitors (conventional DMARDs are ineffective for axial disease). Spinal mobility exercises and physiotherapy are important.

faqs.question: Should I get vaccinated if I have psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Annual influenza vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine, and shingles vaccine (Shingrix) are recommended. Live vaccines (yellow fever, varicella) should be avoided while on biologics or JAK inhibitors. COVID-19 vaccination is recommended. Discuss timing with your rheumatologist.

faqs.question: Can children get psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Juvenile psoriatic arthritis is recognized and accounts for a small proportion of pediatric chronic arthritis. It often features dactylitis, nail changes, and family history of psoriasis. Treatment principles are similar (NSAIDs, methotrexate, biologics) with pediatric rheumatology coordination.

faqs.question: What comorbidities are associated with psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Comorbidities include cardiovascular disease (1.5-fold increase), metabolic syndrome (40-50%), depression (15-25%), uveitis (5-10%), inflammatory bowel disease (5-7%), fatty liver disease, and osteoporosis. Active screening and management of these comorbidities substantially improves overall outcomes and survival.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for psoriatic arthritis?
faqs.answer: With modern treat-to-target therapy, 50-70% of patients achieve substantial response, joint damage is significantly slowed, and life expectancy approaches that of the general population. Early diagnosis and treatment are the strongest predictors of good outcome. Comorbidity management is essential for long-term survival.

faqs.question: How does psoriasis severity affect psoriatic arthritis risk?
faqs.answer: Patients with more severe psoriasis (PASI > 10), nail involvement, scalp psoriasis, and longer disease duration have higher risk of developing psoriatic arthritis. Nail psoriasis triples PsA risk. Aggressive treatment of severe skin disease may reduce subsequent PsA incidence in some studies.

faqs.question: Can psoriatic arthritis go into remission?
faqs.answer: Yes. With effective treatment, 20-30% of patients achieve sustained minimal disease activity or remission. Some patients can taper biologic therapy successfully, although most need ongoing treatment to maintain remission. Discontinuation often leads to flare within 6-12 months.
